coredata.dc:title: Biomimetic ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on perovskite quantum dots and carbon dots for ultrasensitive ciprofloxacin detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Qi
Li, Xiaofei
Xu, Yongzheng
Xu, Zhixiang
Xu, Longhua
coredata.dc:description: The development of high-performance fluorescence (FL) sensing platform for sensitive and selective monitoring ciprofloxacin (CIP) antibiotic residues is crucial for food safety and environmental protection. Herein, we present an innovative dual-response ratiometric FL sensor, fabricated from perovskite quantum dots (PQDs) and carbon dots (CDs) embedded within a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) (PQDs/CDs@MIP) via an efficient one-pot sol-gel method. The constructed PQDs/CDs@MIP sensor combines the specific recognition of MIPs with dual FL emission at 460 nm (CDs) and 518 nm (PQDs). Upon exposure to CIP, the blue FL can be enhanced while the green FL is quenched, and the FL intensity ratio (F460/F518) is linearly dependent on the CIP concentration. Benefiting from the biomimetic molecular recognition and the dual-signaling transduction, the developed sensor demonstrates excellent selectivity and ultra-high sensitivity, achieving a remarkably low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.005 μmol L−1 in a wide concentration range of 0.01–30.0 μmol L−1. The proposed sensor has been successfully applied to the quantitative determination of CIP in real aquatic products, with satisfactory recovery rates of 90.0 %-106.7 %. This work offers a promising new strategy for the development of ratiometric sensing system with dual-signaling modes for monitoring antibiotic residue.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Perovskite quantum dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Carbon dots
Ciprofloxacin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Samples preparation and HPLC method validation
Recovery of CIP in real samples by standard addition method
Morphology and structure characterization of synthesized materials
The photoluminescence properties of PQDs/CDs@MIP toward CIP
Sensing selectivity of PQDs/CDs@MIP toward CIP
Sensing mechanism of PQDs/CDs@MIP toward CIP
Establishment of ratiometric FL sensing analytical method based on PQDs/CDs@MIP for CIP
Ratiometric FL sensing detection of CIP in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32272420), the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. ZR2022MC162), and the Incubation Program for Youth Innovation Team in Shandong Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of PQDs/silica and PQDs/CDs@MIP. TEM images at different magnifications of PQDs/silica (A-C) and PQDs/CDs@MIP (D-F); (G) XRD patterns of PQDs/silica and PQDs/CDs@MIP, (H) FT-IR spectra of CDs, PQDs/silica, PQDs/CDs@MIP and PQDs/CDs@NIP; (I) XPS spectra of PQDs/CDs@MIP.

The influence of synthesized PQDs/CDs@MIPs using different amounts of CDs(A) and different ratios of CIP: APTES: TMOS (B) on the value of K and IF for CIP. (C) PL emission spectra of CDs (black), PQDs (red), PQDs/CDs@MIP in the absence (blue) and presence of CIP (green, 5 μmol L−1) in ethanol. (D) FL response of PQDs/CDs@MIP and PQDs/CDs@NIP toward 5 μmol L−1 CIP at different incubation time.
Selectivity of PQDs/CDs@MIP and PQDs/CDs@NIP toward CIP and its structure analogues at concentration of 5 μmol L−1.

Spectroscopic analysis and fluorescence behavior of PQDs/CDs@MIP. FL decay curves of PQDs in PQDs/CDs@MIP with and without CIP, along with double exponential (A) and three exponential (B) fitting results. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of CIP and PQDs/CDs@MIP, along with fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of PQDs/CDs@MIP. (D) The FT-IR spectra of CIP, PQDs/CDs@MIP in the absence and presence of CIP.

FL and chromaticity response of PQDs/CDs@MIP toward CIP. (A) FL spectra of PQDs/CDs@MIP with increasing concertation of CIP (μmol L−1). (B) FL response of PQDs/CDs@MIP and PQDs/CDs@NIP to varying concentrations of CIP. (C) Linear fitting curves of FL response (FCDs/FPQDs) for PQDs/CDs@MIP and PQDs/CDs@NIP versus CIP concentration. (D) CIE chromaticity diagram and FL shift from green to blue (upper right insert) of PQDs/CDs@MIP with increasing CIP concentrations (0.01–30.0 μmol L−1).

Results comparison for the determination of CIP in aquatic products by our method and the standard HPLC method; ns: no significant difference (p > 0.05). The error bars represent the standard deviation of three parallel measurements.
The fabrication of PQDs/silica (A) and PQDs/CDs@MIP (B) for ratiometric fluorescence sensing detection of CIP.
Recovery results for the determination of CIP in aquatic products using PQDs/CDs@MIP.
The comparison of different analytical methods for the determination of CIP.

row.entry: A ratiometric fluorometric based riboflavin and carbon dots

head.ce:title: Biomimetic ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on perovskite quantum dots and carbon dots for ultrasensitive ciprofloxacin detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai’an, Shandong 271018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai’an, Shandong 271018, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Affiliated Tai’an City Central Hospital of Qingdao University, Tai’an, Shandong 27100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Affiliated Tai’an City Central Hospital of Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Affiliated Tai’an City Central Hospital of Qingdao University, Tai’an, Shandong 27100, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Qi Zhang and Xiaofei Li contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of high-performance fluorescence (FL) sensing platform for sensitive and selective monitoring ciprofloxacin (CIP) antibiotic residues is crucial for food safety and environmental protection. Herein, we present an innovative dual-response ratiometric FL sensor, fabricated from perovskite quantum dots (PQDs) and carbon dots (CDs) embedded within a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) (PQDs/CDs@MIP) via an efficient one-pot sol-gel method. The constructed PQDs/CDs@MIP sensor combines the specific recognition of MIPs with dual FL emission at 460 nm (CDs) and 518 nm (PQDs). Upon exposure to CIP, the blue FL can be enhanced while the green FL is quenched, and the FL intensity ratio (F460/F518) is linearly dependent on the CIP concentration. Benefiting from the biomimetic molecular recognition and the dual-signaling transduction, the developed sensor demonstrates excellent selectivity and ultra-high sensitivity, achieving a remarkably low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.005 μmol L−1 in a wide concentration range of 0.01–30.0 μmol L−1. The proposed sensor has been successfully applied to the quantitative determination of CIP in real aquatic products, with satisfactory recovery rates of 90.0 %-106.7 %. This work offers a promising new strategy for the development of ratiometric sensing system with dual-signaling modes for monitoring antibiotic residue.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ciprofloxacin (CIP), a second-generation fluoroquinolone, is extensively used in humans medicine，livestock production, and aquaculture due to its broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and potent bactericidal effect. However, its environmental persistence, even at low concentrations, has raised significant concerns. These concerns stem primarily from its incomplete metabolism organisms and subsequent environmental accumulation, which may lead to three critical consequences: 1) the emergence of zoonotic CIP-resistant pathogens through antimicrobial resistance selection pressure, 2) potential toxicological impacts and allergic reactions in exposed organisms [1], and 3) disruptions to microbial ecological balance.

Compounding these risks is the widespread and sometimes excessive application of CIP in agriculture and aquaculture practices. This practice increases the likelihood of drug residues transmission through the food chain, thereby exacerbating public health challenges associated with bacterial resistance development [2]. To mitigate these risks, regulatory authorities have established the maximum residue limits (MRLs) for CIP in various animal-derived foods. For example, China’s Ministry of Agriculture and the European Commission have set the MRLs for CIP in fish at 100 μg/kg. Additionally, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration has withdrawn approval for the use of CIP in poultry water treatment to prevent antibiotic-resistant bacteria. These regulatory measures underscore the critical need for rapid and sensitive detection of CIP methods for CIP residues.

Current analytical methods for CIP detection, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3,4], capillary electrophoresis [5], electrochemical-mass spectrometry [6] and the surface-enhanced Raman scattering [7], are effective but suffer from the limitations including dependence on expensive instrumentation, specialized training, and laborious sample preparation, which hinder their applicability for on-site testing [8]. To overcome these challenges, alternative sensing platforms including electrochemical [9], photoelectrochemical [1], immunological [10] and fluorescent [11,12] methods, have been explored. Among these, FL sensors stand out due to their rapid response, operational simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and capacity for high throughput analysis with minimal sample preparation.

Conventional single-emission FL sensors, however, are susceptible to interference from environmental or instrumental variations [13]. In contrast, ratiometric FL sensors, which utilize the dual channel signals at distinct wavelengths, have garnered prominence. These sensors integrate an intrinsic self-calibrating mechanism that compensates for background noise and matrix effects, thereby enhancing the accuracy and reliability of measurement in complex samples [8]. Moreover, the wavelength specific FL color gradients generated by ratiometric system facilitate semiquantitative on-site detection through naked-eye visualization [14]. The construction of ratiometric sensors generally necessitates dual exogenous fluorescent reporters for signal normalization. However, an exceptional paradigm exists when monitoring intrinsically fluorescent analytes, where a single reporter system achieves equivalent calibration efficacy through endogenous signal referencing [15]. Regardless of the situation, the universal implementation of such sensors necessitates fluorescent materials with robust photostability, analyte-responsive emission properties, and compatibility with hybrid nanomaterial systems.

Carbon dots, recognized for their high biocompatibility, tunable surface functionalization, and straightforward synthesis [16,17], have emerged as promising components for ratiometric FL sensors. Their integration with complementary nanomaterials, such as silicon dots [18], quantum dots [19,20], and fluorescent metal-organic framework [21], allows dual-signal referencing for enhanced analytical performance. Despite these advances, existing ratiometric FL sensors still face challenges in selectivity and sensitivity, particularly in distinguishing CIP from structurally similar fluoroquinolone antibiotics. Molecular imprinting polymers (MIPs), often termed “artificial antibodies”, with its specificity and structural stability, offers a promising solution for construction highly sensitive fluorescent sensors [22,23].

To address these challenges, we propose a novel ratiometric FL sensing platform based on perovskite quantum dots (PQDs) and CDs embedded molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs). PQDs, an emerging class of luminescent materials, exhibit exceptional FL properties, including high quantum yield, broad excitation spectra, and narrow emission bands [24–26], but their instability under ambient conditions limits practical use. Encapsulating PQDs within a silica-MIPs layer not only enhances their environment stability but also introduces specific CIP recognition sites. The MIP matrix was synthesized via a one-pot sol-gel method using 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES) as a functional monomer and tetramethoxysilane (TMOS) as a crosslinker, simultaneously embedding silylation-modified PQDs and CDs.

Upon exposure to CIP, the MIPs layer selectively captures the target analyte, inducing a ratiometric response: the blue FL of CDs at 460 nm intensifies, while the green emission of PQDs at 518 nm is quenched. The FL response mechanism of CDs and PQDs for CIP was discussed in detail. The FL intensity ratio (F460 /F518) exhibits a linear correlation with CIP concentration, enabling precise quantification with minimal interference. Notably, the distinct color transition from green to blue permits semiquantitative on-site analysis by visual inspection.

This work represents the first dual-response ratiometric FL biomimetic sensor based on PQDs and CDs embedded in MIPs, offering an innovative solution for rapid, sensitive, and on-site CIP detection. This work not only presents a clever one-pot fabrication strategy for PQDs/CDs@MIP using silane coupling agents, but more importantly, introduces a new CDs-PQDs ratiometric system that allows for a wider range of targets detection by replacing the surface ligands of fluorescent quantum dots.

River shrimp, prawn, crucian carp and hairtail were purchased from a local supermarket in Tai’an, Shandong, China. CIP, norfloxacin (NOR), enrofloxacin (ENR), ofloxacin (OFL), levofloxacin (LEV), fleroxacin (FLE), pefloxacin (PEF), and Cs2CO3 were acquired from Macklin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). PbBr2, oleic acid (OA), octadecene (ODE), TMOS, APTES were supported by Aladdin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-hexane and ethyl alcohol were bought from Guangdong Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd.

The FL spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra were respectively recorded using a LUMINA LF-1701009 (Thermo, America) and a UV-3600 plus UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The crystalline structure of materials was studied by a wide-angle powder diffraction from 5° to 50° on a D8 advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The morphology and structure were observed by a 2100 F transmission electron microscopy (JEM, Japan). The functional groups of PQDs/CDs@MIP were identified using a NICOLET iS10-Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Thermo, America) under the transmission model in 4000–400 cm−1 wavenumbers. The nitrogen physisorption isotherms were measured on an auto-sorb-iQA3200–4 automatic analyzer (Quanta chrome, USA) to give specific surface area and pore distribution information. An ESCALAB250 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo, America) was employed to investigated the element composition.

The organosilane-functionalized CDs and PQD/silica powder were prepared according to the previous report [27–29]. PQDs/CDs@MIP was synthesized by a molecularly imprinted sol-gel process as illustrated in Scheme 1. First, the as-prepared PQDs (30.0 mg), CDs and CIP (0.1 mmol) were uniformly dispersed in 15 mL of ODE by means of ultrasonic treatment. Subsequently, APTES and TMOS were added dropwise to the above mixture at regular intervals under constant magnetic stirring for 15 h at room temperature. The CIP-imprinted composite was centrifuged and washed with ethanol until no CIP was detected. Finally, the PQDs/CDs@MIP was collected by drying overnight.

In contrast, the perovskite quantum dots and carbon dots - embedded molecularly imprinted polymers (PQDs/CDs@NIP), were also prepared in an identical manner in the absence of CIP to verify the effectiveness of the tailor-made imprinting sites introduced into PQDs/CDs@MIP.

To obtain satisfactory performance for ratiometric sensing and imprinted recognition for CIP, the amounts of carbon dots and the ratio of CIP: APTES: TMOS were optimized using the rate constant (K) and the imprinted factor (IF) as the evaluation metrics, respectively. The Stern-Volmer equation for rate constant (K) is:
FCDs/FPQDs =KCCIP -1
Where FCDs and FPQDs are the FL intensity of carbon dots and perovskite quantum dots in PQDs/CDs@MIP or PQDs/CDs@MIP after incubation in CIP standard solution, respectively, CCIP is the concentration of CIP. In addition, the imprinting factor was also obtained by the ratio of the rate constant (K) of PQDs/CDs@MIP(K PQDs/CDs@MIP) or PQDs/CDs@MIP (K PQDs/CDs@NIP), that is,
IF=K PQDs/CDs@MIP/K PQDs/CDs@NIP

ce:section.ce:section-title: Samples preparation and HPLC method validation
ce:section.ce:para: The purchased river shrimp, prawn, crucian carp and hairtail were used as samples to evaluate the practical performance of the developed PQDs/CDs@MIP sensor. The Chinese national standard method (GB/T 31656.3–2021) was adopted for sample pretreatment and method validation, and the detailed procedure was provided in the supplementary materials.

The ratiometric FL sensing procedure for CIP was performed as follows: 2.0 mg of PQDs/CDs@MIP or PQDs/CDs@NIP powder was added to 3.0 mL of ethanol solution containing varying amounts of CIP. The mixture was incubated at room temperature (25 °C) for with gentle shaking at 200 rpm for 25 mins. The FL emission spectra were recorded under excited at 365 nm, and the ratio of F460 nm/F518 nm were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of CIP in real samples by standard addition method
ce:section.ce:para: Recovery experiment was conducted using prawn-1 and crucian carp samples. Prior to spiking, background levels of CIP in the samples were determined by HPLC. A series of CIP standard solutions (0.3 mL) at different concentrations (0.1, 0.3, and 1.0 μmol L−1) were spiked into each sample (15.0 g). After homogenization, the samples were stored overnight at 4°C. Subsequently, the samples were extracted and dissolved with ethanol solution (3 mL), to achieve the spiked concentrations of 0.01 μmol L−1, 0.03 μmol L−1, and 0.1 μmol L−1 in the extract solution, and then analyzed using FL sensing detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of synthesized materials
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of PQDs/silica and PQDs/CDs@MIP were observed by TEM images with different magnifications. Given that APTES can be directly silanized without the need for a catalyst [30]. At the initial stage of the reaction, the APTES acts as a capping agent to form the APTES-capped PQDs. Following exposure to the air, APTES functions as a silane coupling agent, gradually forming the organic silica- wrapped PQDs (PQDs/silica) due to the slow hydrolysis of APTES. As illustrated in Fig. 1, the cubic structure of PQDs/silica, exhibiting good dispersion, is observed at the edges of the TEM images (Fig. 1A). Fig. 1B further confirms the successful embedding of PQDs in the silica matrix, consistent with the previous literature [28]. During the imprinted process, the APTES acts as a functional monomer, forming recognition sites via hydrogen bonding interaction with CIP. The resulting PQDs/CDs@MIP presents a well-defined core-shell spherical structure, consisting of a 60 nm thick silicon-based MIP outer shell (transparent) and a central core formed by PQDs/silica aggregation (Fig. 1C-D). In contrast, PQDs/CDs@NIP has a similar core-shell structure but with a thicker outer shell (Fig. 1E-F). This difference may be attributed to the absence of template molecules in the non-imprinted polymer preparation, which eliminates spatial hindrance and facilitates the polymerization process.

The XRD patterns of PQDs/silica and PQDs/CDs@MIP are shown in Fig. 1G. The diffraction peaks located at 2θ values of 15.2°, 21.5°, 30.4°, 34.2° and 37.7° are corresponding to the (100), (110), (200), (210) and (211) crystal planes of the PQDs/silica [31], which is consistent with the simulated XRD spectra of PQDs (CsPbBr3, PDF#54–0752), demonstrating the thin silica shell doesn’t change the crystal structure of PQDs. Notably, these characteristic diffraction peaks of PQDs essentially disappear in the spectrum of PQDs/CDs@MIP, and some new peaks appear at 2θ= 20°-25°, which can be ascribed to the encapsulation of the thick amorphous silica-based imprinted shell, further confirming the successful preparation of silica-based MIP on PQDs.

To investigate the functional groups of the fabricated materials, the FT-IR analysis for PQDs, PQDs/CDs@MIP before and after elution of CIP and PQDs/CDs@NIP was implemented and their spectra are recorded. As displayed in Fig. 1H, the characteristic peak at 3310 cm−1 results from the telescopic vibration of O-H or N-H, while the absorption peak at 2963 cm−1 is due to the asymmetric stretching vibration of the C-H bond [32]. Notably, the PQDs/CDs@MIP composites have many characteristic peaks associated with CDs and PQDs, with the absorption peak at 1405 cm−1 in PQDs/CDs@MIP originating from the vibration of -COO in the carboxyl group of CDs [33,34], whereas the absorption peak at 1540 cm−1 is mainly from the stretching of CC in the PQDs [35]. The absorption peaks at 1024 and 952 cm−1 are contributed by the Si-O-Si and Si-O groups [36,37], which implies the presence of APTES and TMOS. In addition, no significant discrepancy was identified between the MIP and NIP spectra in terms of the positioning of characteristic peaks, indicating the effective removal of CIP. Above results again validated the successful preparation of PQDs/CDs@MIP.

The elemental composition and chemical states in PQDs/CDs@MIP were also determined by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1I, the existence of Cs, O, N, C, Pb, Si and Br elements is evidenced by a shoulder on the main peaks at 739.1, 723.5, 533, 286.5, 140.2, 103.6 and 68.7 eV, which is assigned to Cs 3d, O 1 s, C 1 s, Pb 4 f, Si 2p and Br 3d species. The high resolution XPS peaks of Cs 3d (B), Pb 4 f (C), Br 3d (D), Si2p (E) and O 1 s (F) was also displayed in Fig. S1, the characteristic peaks of the binding energy of 712.3 eV and 726.3 eV are attributed to Cs 3d5/2 and Cs 3d3/2 in Cs 3d (in Fig. S1A). The Pb 4 f XPS spectrum (Fig. S1B)) has wo peaks at 131.5 eV and 137 eV, which correspond to the signals of Pb 4 f7/2 and Pb 4 f5/2. The two peaks centered at 67 eV and 68.5 eV in Fig. S1C for Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2 can be ascribed to the Br-Pb and Br-H bonds, respectively. The peaks at 103.5 eV and 102.7 eV in Fig. S1D are attributed as Si-O and Si-C. Additionally, the Fig. 1E shows that the peaks of 531.0 eV and 530.5 eV are caused by C-O bond. As a result, the PQDs/CDs@MIP has been synthesized successfully with an expected composition based on the above XPS analyses.

Meanwhile, the porosity of PQDs/CDs@MIP and PQDs/CDs@NIP were clarified by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (Fig. S1F). PQDs/CDs@MIP and PQDs/CDs@NIP shows a typical type-I isotherm characteristics of a microporous solid. The BET surface area and pore volume for PQDs/CDs@MIP were 147 m2g−1 and 0.158 cm3g−1, whereas these are 79 m2g−1 and 0.087 cm3g−1 for PQDs/CDs@NIP. A higher surface area and a larger pore volume in PQDs/CDs@MIP than PQDs/CDs@NIP implied the existence of imprinted cavities produced from the pre-addition and post-removal of CIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photoluminescence properties of PQDs/CDs@MIP toward CIP
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that the composition of PQDs/CDs@MIP significantly affects its sensing performance, the usage of carbon dots and the ratio of template molecule CIP, functional monomer APTES and the crosslinker TMOS was optimized based on the rate constant (K) and imprinting factor (IF) as evaluation metrics. The results in Fig. 2A and B show that when using 500 μL of CDs and a CIP: APTES: TMOS ratio of 1:5:20, the resulting PQDs/CDs@MIP achieved the highest K and IF values of 0.0042 and 2.57, corresponding to the optimal sensing sensitivity and specific recognition of CIP by PQDs/CDs@MIP.

As shown in Fig. 2C, the PQDs/CDs@MIP dispersed in ethanol exhibits the obvious emission at 460 nm and 518 nm under excited at 365 nm UV light, which is in well accordance with the pure CDs and PQDs, suggesting that the MIP encapsulation didn’t alter the FL propriety of the single material. And the photostability of the as-synthesized PQDs/CDs@MIP, as one of the most important parameters that determines the actual performance, was investigated by monitoring their respective emission intensity exposed to the natural light in different times. The results in Fig. S2 showed the FL intensity did not undergo a particularly obvious change during 0–168 h, suggesting its good anti-photobleaching and good FL stability resulted from the protective effect of MIP layer for both quantum dots, which lays an important foundation for its application in subsequent complex solvents environments. Fig. 2C also presents the favorable ratiometric sensing features that can be expected for PQDs/CDs@MIP. Upon the appearance of the CIP, the green FL at 518 nm will be quenched, while the blue FL at 460 nm is obviously enhanced. Therefore, in this protocol, a double-signaling response sensing system is promising to be established based on the ratio of their FL intensity (FCDs/FPQDs).

The FL response intensity (FCDs/FPQDs) of PQDs/CDs@MIP toward 5 μmol L−1 CIP at different binding time were explored at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 2D, upon addition of CIP, the FCDs/FPQDs value in PQDs/CDs@MIP exhibited a linear increase during the initial 20 mins. This rapid rise is attribute to the large number of template molecules diffusing into the imprinted polymer network, anchoring at the recognition sites, and subsequently interacting with the encapsulated fluorescent CDs and PQDs, thereby generating a significant enhancement in the FL response. Notably, within just 5 mins, the FCDs/FPQDs value reached 65.3 % of its maximum, and it stabilized at 0.69 after 25 mins. This indicates the rapid FL response of PQDs/CDs@MIP to CIP, which is well-suited for field application. Furthermore, PQDs/CDs@MIP demonstrated a higher equilibrium value and a faster response rate toward CIP compared to PQDs/CDs@NIP, primarily due to the presence of CIP-imprinted sites, which facilitate a more efficient and accessible pathway.

The FL response of PQDs/CDs@MIP toward CIP (10 μmol L⁻¹) was evaluated at different temperatures (5°C, 15°C, 25°C, 35°C, and 45°C). As illustrated in Fig. S3, no significant variations in FL response were observed, with an RSD of 2.2 %. This indicates that, compared with the traditional enzyme-substrate interactions, the binding between the MIP and the target molecule largely eliminates temperature dependence, thereby demonstrating superior environmental adaptability.

Additionally, PQDs/CDs@MIP were recovered by centrifugation at 8000 r/min for 10 mins and washed three times with a regeneration reagent consisting of ethanol and acetic acid (3 mL, v/v = 8:2). As shown in Fig. S4, after five cycles, the FCDs/FPQDs value of PQDs/CDs@MIP increased to 109 % of its initial value, likely due to a reduction in PQDs FL caused by acetic acid treatment. Importantly, the FL response (FCDs/FPQDs) of PQDs/CDs@MIP toward 10 μmol L⁻¹ CIP remained stable, with the sixth cycle maintaining 102.5 % of the original response. These results highlight the excellent recyclability and robustness of PQDs/CDs@MIP.

In light of the established toxicity of lead element, the cytotoxicity of PQDs/CDs@MIP nanocomposites were evaluated using a MTT assay with HepG2 cells. The effects of different concentrations of PQDs/CDs@MIP treated for 12 and 24 h on the activity of HepG2 cells are illustrated in Fig. 3A. As the concentration of PQDs/CDs@MIP varied from 0 to 5000 μg mL−1, a decline in cell viability was observed, which then plateaued. After an incubation period of 12 h and 24 h with PQDs/CDs@MIP, 95 % and 90 % of cells survived, respectively, thus demonstrating the low level of toxicity of the PQDs/CDs@MIP composites. It is noteworthy that the designed test PQDs/CDs@MIP concentration and incubation times were considerably higher than those employed in real applications, a result that is consistent with the hypothesis that inert material encapsulation effectively prevents the release of toxic elements and reduces the PQDs toxicity of PQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing selectivity of PQDs/CDs@MIP toward CIP
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the sensing system was evaluated by measuring the FL response of PQDs/CDs@MIP toward 5 μmol L−1 CIP and its structural analogues, including enrofloxacin (ENR), ofloxacin (OFLX), levofloxacin (LEV), fleroxacin (FO), and pefloxacin (PEF), whose structures are provided in Fig. S5 for reference. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, after 25 min of incubation, target molecular CIP elicited the most pronounced FL response (0.72) from PQDs/CDs@MIP, followed by levofloxacin (0.135), pefloxacin (0.131), fleroxacin, enrofloxacin and the minimum ofloxacin (0.056). This indicates that the tailor-made MIP material is high compatible with the target molecule, rather than with the other analogues. The formation of CIP-imprinted cavities was enabled by hydrogen bond interactions between the carboxylic group of CIP and the amino group of the functional monomer APTES. This resulted in the generation of CIP-imprinted cavities that exhibited a markedly enhanced affinity and preferential selectivity for CIP during the adsorption process. However, the lack of complete matching between the shape, size and functional group of the antibiotics and the recognition sites in PQDs/CDs@MIP presented a significant challenge in capturing these analogs. Furthermore, in comparison to PQDs/CDs@MIP, PQDs/CDs@NIP demonstrated a diminished FCDs/FPQDs value in the presence of analytes, which can be ascribed to the absence of an accessible recognition site. It is noteworthy that, unlike the results of conventional adsorption experiments, the FL response of the non-imprinted polymer to the target molecule is also significantly higher than that of other structural analogues. This could be attributed to the selective enhancement achieved through the utilisation of dual-signaling response. The results implied that not only the imprinting recognition but more importantly the dual single transduction confer the ultrahigh selectivity of PQDs/CDs@MIP for CIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of PQDs/CDs@MIP toward CIP
ce:section.ce:para: The FL quenching mechanism of PQDs in PQDs/CDs@MIP was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4A, no overlap was observed between the UV absorption spectrum of CIP and the excitation/emission spectra of PQDs/CDs@MIP. Consequently, the FL resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect and FL internal rate effect (IFE) were initially excluded as potential mechanisms. Then, the FL decay of PQDs/CDs@MIP at 518 nm was respectively measured in the absence and presence of CIP. A double exponential (Fig. 4B) and a three-exponential (Fig. 4C) fitting analysis was performed, suggesting they follows well three exponential emission behavior. Therefore, the average FL lifetime of PQDs/CDs@MIP was calculated using the three-exponential fitting function, yielding a value of 79.5 ns. After adding CIP, this lifetime significantly decreased to be 22.3 ns, confirming a dynamic quenching process. Considering the overlap of the UV absorption peaks of CIP and PQDs in PQDs/CDs@MIP (Fig. 4C), electron transfer from PQDs to CIP is proposed as the primary quenching mechanism.

Simultaneously, the principle behind the FL enhancement of carbon dots in PQDs/CDs@MIP was deduced. To evaluate the contribution of CIP's FL on FL enhancement of the mixed system, the FL emission spectra of CIP standard solutions at concentrations of 5, 10, and 30 μmol L⁻¹ were measured. As shown in Fig. S6, the FL emission of CIP increased as its concentration rose, and overlapped with that of the carbon dots. However, compared to the FL enhancement induced by the interaction of CDs and CIP, the effect of CIP's own FL emission was negligible. Therefore, the observed FL enhancement in the system is attributed to the interaction between CDs and CIP. Given the presence of numerous hydrophilic groups (-NH2, -OH, and -COOH) on the surface of CDs in PQDs/CDs@MIP and a variety of C-F, C-N, CO, and COOH groups on the surface of CIP, it can be postulated that strong hydrogen bond interactions between the entrapped CIP and the CD surfaces result in FL enhancement. This hypothesis was supported by the results of the FT-IR experiment shown in Fig. 4D. Compared to the infrared vibrational peak at about 3500 nm for C-OH in CIP and PQDs/CDs@MIP, a broadened peak with an evident red shift was observed for C-OH in the mixed system of CIP and PQDs/CDs@MIP, indicating hydrogen bond formation [38]. The CIP functions as a passivator by reducing the surface defects of carbon dots, thereby improving their luminescence efficiency and increasing the FL of PQDs/CDs@MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of ratiometric FL sensing analytical method based on PQDs/CDs@MIP for CIP
ce:section.ce:para: The dose-dependent interaction between the FL response (FCDs/FPQDs) and CIP concentration was revealed by introducing PQDs/CDs@MIP or PQDs/CDs@NIP into various concentrations of CIP. As the CIP concentration increased, the characteristic emission peak of CDs at 460 nm progressively intensified, whereas the emission peak of PQDs at 518 nm continuously diminished under 365 nm excitation ( Fig. 5A). The FL response (FCDs/FPQDs) in both PQDs/CDs@MIP and PQDs/CDs@NIP exhibited a clear dependence on CIP concentration over an ultrawide range of 0.01–200.0 μmol L−1 (Fig. 5B).

A satisfactory linear relationship, F
CDs
/F
PQDs = 0.062 C
CIP + 0.406, was observed for PQDs/CDs@MIP in the range of 0.01–30 μmol L−1 CIP after 25 mins of incubation (Fig. 5C). The limit of detection (LOD) for CIP was calculated to be 0.005 μmol L−1, based on the standard 3σ/k. In contrast, PQDs/CDs@NIP displayed a weaker FL response due to the absence of an imprinted recognition unit, with a linear equation of F
CDs
/F
PQDs = 0.0145 C
CIP + 0.441. The imprinted factor was determined as 4.27, calculated as the ratio of the two standard curves, further affirming the high selectivity of the developed ratiometric sensing method for CIP. Moreover, the emission color evolution of the ratiometric PQDs/CDs@MIP probe was readily discernible to the naked eye under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 5D insert), where the color shifted from green to blue as the CIP concentration increased. This observation was consistent with the CIE chromaticity coordinates (Fig. 5D), thereby offering a convenient visual analysis for CIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric FL sensing detection of CIP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the feasibility of the established PQDs/CDs@MIP sensing method, spiking and recovery experiments were conducted using prawn-1 and crucian carp samples. Prior to spiking, the background levels of CIP in the samples were determined to 0.015 mg kg−1 and 0.020 mg kg−1 by the Chinese national standard method. These values correspond to CIP concentrations in the extracted solution of 0.229 μmol L−1and 0.302 μmol L−1, respectively. A satisfactory recovery rate ranging from 90.0 % to 106.7 % were obtained at the spiked levels at 0.01, 0.03 and 0.1 μmol L−1 in Table 1, which implies that the constructed ratiometric FL sensing system is particularly suited to the quantitative analysis of CIP in real samples.

To validate the accuracy of the developed method, it was also applied to monitor CIP levels in other aquatic samples, including river shrimp, prawn-2 and hairtail. The detected contents were further validated by the Chinese National Standard HPLC method (GB/T 31656.3–2021). A paired t-test was conducted to compare the results from the developed method and the standard HPLC method, and no significant differences were observed (p > 0.05) in Fig.6 . This statistical analysis further confirms the high accuracy and reliability of the developed method for CIP detection in real samples.

To highlight the superior performance of our developed sensing system, we compared it with previously reported methods in Table 2. When compared to earlier quantum dots-based FL sensing, or riboflavin and carbon dots-based ratiometric FL sensing approaches for CIP detection [18,39], our constructed PQDs/CDs@MIP-based ratiometric FL sensing system demonstrated enhanced sensitivity with a lower LOD. Notably, our developed PQDs/CDs@MIP sensor achieved a much lower LOD than surface-enhanced Raman scattering, and even showed comparable performance to electrochemical sensors, but with a wider liner range [40,41]. Furthermore, the method is also rapid, cost-effective, and adaptable, making it a promising tool for applications in food safety, environmental monitoring, and other fields where high-throughput and precise analysis are crucial.

The superiority of PQDs/CDs@MIP sensor can be attributed to two key factors: (i) the dual-emitting CDs and PQDs provide stable FL output and reverse signal variations due to the synergistic effects of hydrogen-bonding interactions and electron transfer, enhancing both sensitivity and accuracy; (ii) The CIP-imprinted sites enable efficient target molecule capture, reducing pre-processing and analysis time while increasing selectivity. Overall, this sensor offers an efficient, effective solution for sensitive CIP detection in complex samples.

We have proposed a novel biomimetic ratiometric sensor by combination of CDs, PQDs and MIP. As a proof of concept, the developed PQDs/CDs@MIP sensor has been successfully applied for the highly sensitive and selective detection of CIP in aquatic product samples over a wide range of 0.01–30 μmol L−1, with a LOD as low as 0.005 μmol L−1. This work offers a simple and effective strategy to enhance detection sensitivity through the integration of efficient molecular recognition and unique dual-signaling ratiometric sensing. More importantly, it paves the way for the engineering application of PQDs in complex environments.

Ciprofloxacin, a widely used antibiotic, is commonly found in various environmental media. The persistent presence of CIP residues can disrupt biological metabolic processes and contribute to the development of bacterial resistance, posing significant risks to human health and ecological safety. This study introduces an innovative biomimetic ratiometric FL sensor, incorporating perovskite quantum dots and carbon dots, specifically designed for ultrasensitive detection of CIP residues. The dual-response FL mechanism enhances detection accuracy in complex food systems, thereby supporting routine monitoring and regulatory compliance. This work advances food safety and promotes sustainable environmental monitoring to reduce health risks associated with antibiotic residues.

Xu Longhua: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xu Zhixiang: Supervision, Conceptualization. Xu Yongzheng: Validation, Investigation. Li Xiaofei: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software. Zhang Qi: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32272420), the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. ZR2022MC162), and the Incubation Program for Youth Innovation Team in Shandong Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2025.137845.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface decoration of cadmium-sulfide quantum dots with 3-mercaptopropionic acid as a fluorescence probe for determination of ciprofloxacin in real samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Madrakian, S. Maleki, A. Afkhami, Surface decoration of cadmium-sulfide quantum dots with 3-mercaptopropionic acid as a fluorescence probe for determination of ciprofloxacin in real samples, Sensor Actuat B-Chem. 243 (2017) 14-21, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.11.106.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of ciprofloxacin in fish by surface-enhanced Raman scattering using a liquid-liquid self-assembled gold nanofilm

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, Y. Teng, Y. Qin, Z. Ren, Z. Wang, Determination of ciprofloxacin in fish by surface-enhanced Raman scattering using a liquid-liquid self-assembled gold nanofilm, Anal. Lett. 53(4), (2020) 660-670. https://doi.org/10.1080/00032719.2019.1663861.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective electrochemical detection of ciprofloxacin with a porous nafion/multiwalled carbon nanotube composite film electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Gayen, B.P. Chaplin, Selective electrochemical detection of ciprofloxacin with a porous nafion/multiwalled carbon nanotube composite film electrode, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 8(3) (2016) 1615-1626. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.5b07337.
